edges.evidence: The 2024 model collapse paper studies the same recursive-training failure mode formalized in The Curse of Recursion and advances it with broader empirical and theoretical analysis.
The Nature paper builds on Self-Consuming Generative Models go MAD by examining how repeated training on synthetic data degrades future generations of models.
The ESL plagiarism paper extends detector-evasion concerns into an educational assessment setting by discussing AI-text detection for student writing.
The academic cheating paper extends the plagiarism-detection discussion from ESL writing to broader higher-education policy and responsible human-AI collaboration.
The 2024 bypass study directly continues the line of work showing that lightweight transformations such as paraphrasing can evade AI-text detectors.

The 2024 study concludes that simple editing strategies can readily defeat major GenAI detectors, opposing the more optimistic premise that such detectors can be used to control AI-assisted plagiarism.
Can AI-Generated Text be Reliably Detected? builds on prior evidence that detector-based approaches are brittle, including the finding that paraphrasing can defeat detectors.

Can AI-Generated Text be Reliably Detected? argues that reliable detection is fundamentally difficult, which conflicts with the claim that AI-based detectors can effectively police plagiarism in writing tasks.
Principle-Driven Self-Alignment also relies on model-generated supervision for alignment, building on the self-bootstrapping instruction paradigm demonstrated by Self-Instruct.
The human-in-the-loop RL survey explicitly situates newer preference-optimization methods such as DPO within the broader landscape of feedback-driven alignment methods.
The survey builds on RLAIF vs. RLHF as a representative example of replacing expensive human feedback with model-based feedback in alignment pipelines.
RLAIF vs. RLHF extends minimal-human-supervision alignment by replacing much of the human preference signal with scalable AI feedback during reinforcement learning.
AI Supported Degradation of the Self Concept extends concerns about whose opinions language models reflect by theorizing how feedback-driven sycophancy can shape users' self-concepts.
StudentLife uses smartphone-based passive sensing to study behavior and wellbeing, directly following the mobile sensing paradigm introduced by Reality Mining.
OpenAssistant extends the self-instruction trend by broadening it from synthetic instruction generation to a large open human-curated conversational alignment dataset.
WizardLM's Evol-Instruct method starts from instruction-following data generation ideas that were popularized by Self-Instruct and then increases instruction complexity.
